text.title: Approval Token Must Be Signed and Bound
text.summary: Human approval is a signed, narrowly scoped JWT — never a Slack reactji or a string flag.

section.title: JWT claims bind tool, target, incident, and TTL
section.p: An approval token is a short-lived JWT issued by the approval service after a verified human action (Slack callback with OAuth user, MFA challenge, signed UI callback). The executor validates the signature and the claim set on every T2 call.

rule.statement: An approval token MUST be a JWT signed by the approval service; MUST contain `sub` (approver), `tool`, `target`, `incident_id`, `iat`, `exp` (≤ 300 seconds after `iat`), and `jti`; MUST be single-use (server-side `jti` revocation on first acceptance); and MUST be rejected when any claim mismatches the actual call's `(tool, target, incident_id)` arguments.
rule.rationale: An unscoped or replayable token degrades to a "global yes". Binding to the exact `(tool, target, incident)` triple stops a captured token from being reused on a different cluster, and the 5-minute TTL stops it from being held for a future incident.

example.note: One token, one tool, one target, one incident, 5-minute window, single-use jti.
An emoji and a string are not cryptographic evidence. Any process that posts as Alice or knows the string can authorize a delete.

antipattern.description: Reusing the same approval token across "follow-up" actions in the same incident.
antipattern.reason: Each T2 action is a separate decision; "I approved a pod delete" is not "I approved a namespace delete" thirty seconds later.

section.reference: Canonical claim set the executor must validate.
Microsoft Agent Governance Toolkit — signed approvals.
SOC2 compliance posture for AI agent approvals.
